title: The 20-Minute Whole Blood Clotting Test (WBCT) for Snake Bite
pearl: In rural settings without coagulation labs, the 20-min WBCT is your best friend for detecting viper envenomation. Place 2 mL fresh blood in a clean dry glass tube. If clot does not form within 20 minutes, systemic envenomation with coagulopathy is confirmed and ASV is indicated.
explanation: Viper venom contains procoagulant enzymes that consume clotting factors, causing consumptive coagulopathy (DIC). The WBCT is highly sensitive (>90%) for significant viper envenomation and requires no laboratory equipment. Repeat every 6 hours for 24 hours post-ASV to confirm clotting restoration.
references: WHO Guidelines for the Clinical Management of Snake Bite 2016

title: STEMI Equivalent: Posterior MI on Standard 12-lead ECG
pearl: Posterior MI (circumflex/RCA territory) shows ST depression in V1-V3 with dominant R waves — the mirror image of posterior STEMI. Always do posterior leads (V7-V9) in any patient with chest pain and ST depression in V1-V3. ST elevation ≥0.5mm in V7-V9 confirms posterior STEMI — treat same as anterior STEMI.
explanation: Standard ECGs miss posterior MI because no lead directly overlies the posterior wall. The 'reciprocal' changes in V1-V3 (tall R, ST depression, upright T) represent the mirror image of posterior STEMI. This is especially common with inferior MI (check right-sided leads for RV involvement simultaneously).

title: Gentamicin Once-Daily Dosing is Safer than TID
pearl: Once-daily gentamicin (5-7 mg/kg/day) achieves higher peak concentrations (concentration-dependent killing) with equivalent or superior efficacy and LESS nephrotoxicity than traditional TID dosing. The post-antibiotic effect allows drug-free intervals that protect the kidney.
explanation: Aminoglycosides kill bacteria via concentration-dependent mechanism — higher peaks kill more bacteria. The post-antibiotic effect (PAE) means bacteria remain suppressed even when drug levels drop. Once-daily dosing exploits this while allowing renal tubular cells to recover during the drug-free trough period. Exception: endocarditis synergy (use TID low-dose).

title: Magnesium Toxicity Before Eclampsia Prophylaxis Kills — Know the Antidote
pearl: Before giving MgSO4 for eclampsia prophylaxis, always check: RR ≥12/min, knee reflex present, urine output ≥25 mL/hr. If any sign of toxicity (loss of DTRs first, then respiratory depression), STOP Mg and give calcium gluconate 1g IV over 10 min immediately.
explanation: Magnesium levels: Therapeutic 4-7 mEq/L → Loss of DTRs at 7-10 mEq/L → Respiratory arrest at 10-13 mEq/L → Cardiac arrest at >15 mEq/L. Calcium gluconate works as a competitive antagonist. Always keep a 10 mL syringe of 10% calcium gluconate at the bedside whenever MgSO4 is running.

title: Mid-Upper Arm Circumference (MUAC) is the Fastest SAM Screen
pearl: MUAC <11.5 cm in children 6-59 months = Severe Acute Malnutrition (SAM) regardless of weight-for-height. MUAC 11.5-12.5 cm = Moderate AM (MAM). A colored MUAC tape (red/yellow/green) is the most efficient community-level screening tool.
explanation: MUAC correlates strongly with mortality risk and is simpler than weight-for-height z-scores. SAM children with MUAC <11.5 cm have 9x higher mortality risk. MUAC is also less affected by edema or dehydration artifacts. All SAM children need emergency referral; stabilize hypoglycemia (10% dextrose 5 mL/kg) before transport.

title: A Normal D-dimer Does NOT Rule Out PE in High Pre-Test Probability
pearl: D-dimer has high sensitivity (>95%) but LOW specificity. Use it ONLY in LOW to INTERMEDIATE pre-test probability (Wells PE score <4). In high pre-test probability (Wells >4), go straight to CTPA — a negative D-dimer does NOT rule out PE.
explanation: D-dimer is elevated in many conditions (infection, malignancy, surgery, pregnancy, DVT). Its value lies in ruling OUT PE when pre-test probability is low. The Wells PE criteria assigns points for: clinical signs of DVT, PE most likely diagnosis, HR >100, immobilization, previous PE/DVT, hemoptysis, malignancy. High score = proceed directly to imaging.

title: Metformin Should Be Withheld Before IV Contrast — But Only for 48 Hours
pearl: Hold metformin on the day of IV contrast administration and for 48 hours after, then restart if renal function is stable. This is NOT because metformin causes contrast nephropathy — it's because metformin + contrast-induced AKI can precipitate life-threatening lactic acidosis.
explanation: Metformin is renally excreted. Contrast-induced AKI reduces clearance, leading to metformin accumulation. High metformin levels → mitochondrial complex I inhibition → anaerobic metabolism → lactic acidosis. Risk is highest in patients with baseline CKD (eGFR <60). Always check baseline creatinine before elective contrast studies.

title: Dengue NS1 Antigen vs. IgM: Timing is Everything
pearl: NS1 antigen is positive in days 1-5 of fever (acute viremia). IgM antibody becomes positive from day 5-6 onwards. A negative NS1 on day 3 does NOT rule out dengue. If suspected dengue persists beyond day 5, test IgM instead.
explanation: NS1 is shed into blood during active viral replication. After viremia clears (~day 5), NS1 drops but IgM rises. If a patient presents early and NS1 is negative on day 1-2, repeat on day 3-4 or test both. Platelet count nadir typically occurs around day 5-7. Watch for dengue shock syndrome (pulse pressure <20 mmHg, cold extremities) regardless of platelet count.

title: The FAST-ED Score Identifies Large Vessel Occlusion Strokes
pearl: FAST-ED (Face, Arm, Speech, Time — Eyes, Denial) adds gaze deviation and neglect to FAST. A score ≥4 suggests large vessel occlusion (LVO) that may benefit from mechanical thrombectomy up to 24 hours — refer directly to thrombectomy-capable center, bypassing hospitals without intervention capability.
explanation: LVO strokes (MCA, ICA, basilar) have much worse prognosis with thrombolytics alone but respond dramatically to mechanical thrombectomy. NIHSS ≥6 or FAST-ED ≥4 at first clinical contact should prompt direct transfer to thrombectomy center. Time from onset to recanalization ('time is brain' — 1.9 million neurons/minute lost in LVO).

title: Anaphylaxis: Epinephrine First, Antihistamines Later
pearl: In anaphylaxis, IM epinephrine (0.5 mg = 0.5 mL of 1:1000, anterolateral thigh) is the ONLY life-saving drug. Antihistamines and steroids are adjuncts that do NOT treat airway edema or shock — never delay epinephrine to give antihistamines first.
explanation: Anaphylaxis causes mast cell degranulation → histamine, leukotrienes, prostaglandins. Epinephrine reverses all components: α1 vasoconstriction reverses shock; β1 inotropic effect supports heart; β2 bronchodilation opens airways. Antihistamines block histamine but cannot reverse leukotrienes or prostaglandins — they work too slowly for acute anaphylaxis. Second dose of epi every 5-15 min if no response.

title: Inferior MI: Always Check Right Leads for RV Infarction
pearl: Any inferior STEMI (ST elevation in II, III, aVF) → immediately perform right-sided leads (V4R, V5R). ST elevation ≥1mm in V4R confirms RV infarction. These patients are preload-dependent — AVOID nitrates and diuretics which cause severe hypotension. Give IV fluid instead.
explanation: RV infarction complicates 30-50% of inferior MIs. Clinical triad: hypotension + clear lungs + elevated JVP. The RV depends on preload for output. Nitrates reduce preload → precipitate catastrophic hypotension. Fluid resuscitation (500 mL NS bolus) is first-line. Dopamine if fluid-unresponsive. IABP if cardiogenic shock develops.

title: FENa <1% Does Not Always Mean Pre-renal AKI
pearl: FENa (fractional excretion of sodium) <1% suggests pre-renal AKI — BUT it can also be low in contrast nephropathy, myoglobinuria, acute GN, and early obstruction. In patients on diuretics, use FEUrea instead (FEUrea <35% = pre-renal). Clinical context always overrides a single biomarker.
explanation: FENa = (urine Na × plasma Cr) / (plasma Na × urine Cr) × 100. Pre-renal: kidneys avidly retain sodium → FENa <1%. ATN: tubular injury impairs Na reabsorption → FENa >2%. Exceptions: diuretics force natriuresis regardless of volume status → falsely elevated FENa. Use FEUrea in these cases as urea reabsorption is diuretic-independent.

title: Fever Without Focus: Malaria Test Before Antibiotics in Endemic Areas
pearl: In malaria-endemic areas (most of rural India), always perform a malaria RDT or peripheral smear BEFORE starting antibiotics for undifferentiated fever. Empiric antibiotics mask malaria while allowing fatal progression. Typhoid and malaria can coexist.
explanation: Malaria RDTs detect HRP2 (P. falciparum) or pLDH (all species) with >95% sensitivity. A negative RDT in high-suspicion cases needs thick smear confirmation. Treat falciparum immediately with artemisinin-based combination therapy (ACT) — delay >24h in severe malaria carries >20% mortality. Follow NVBDCP guidelines for species-appropriate treatment.

title: Fluoroquinolones Chelate with Divalent Cations — Space Them Out
pearl: Ciprofloxacin/levofloxacin absorption is reduced by 50-90% when taken with antacids (calcium, magnesium, aluminum), iron supplements, zinc, or dairy. Give fluoroquinolones 2 hours BEFORE or 6 hours AFTER these products.
explanation: Fluoroquinolones form insoluble chelate complexes with divalent and trivalent cations in the gut, dramatically reducing bioavailability. This is a common cause of fluoroquinolone treatment failure in patients on antacids or iron supplements. The problem is avoidable with simple timing education — counsel patients explicitly.

title: PPH Rule of 30: When to Act
pearl: Postpartum hemorrhage is >500 mL blood loss after vaginal delivery. The 'Rule of 30' for shock: HR >30 above normal, RR >30, urine output <30 mL/hr, Hb drop of >30%. Start uterotonics at delivery, not when PPH occurs. Oxytocin 10 IU IM is first-line prophylaxis for all deliveries.
explanation: PPH is the #1 cause of maternal mortality in India. 4Ts: Tone (uterine atony, 80%), Trauma, Tissue (retained placenta), Thrombin (coagulopathy). Bimanual uterine compression + oxytocin first. If fails: misoprostol 800 mcg sublingual or ergometrine 0.5 mg IM. Tranexamic acid 1g IV within 3 hours of PPH onset reduces mortality. Use resuscitative hysterectomy if bleeding uncontrolled.

title: CURB-65 ≥3: Hospital Admission Required for CAP
pearl: CURB-65 for community-acquired pneumonia: Confusion, Urea >7 mmol/L, RR ≥30, BP <90/60, Age ≥65. Score 0-1: outpatient. Score 2: consider admission. Score ≥3: hospital. Score ≥4: consider ICU. If urea unavailable, use CRB-65 (Confusion, RR, BP, Age ≥65) — same thresholds.
explanation: CURB-65 predicts 30-day mortality. Score 0=0.7%, 1=2.1%, 2=9.2%, 3=14.5%, 4=40%, 5=57%. Blood cultures should be taken before antibiotics in all hospitalized patients. Cover atypicals (azithromycin or doxycycline) in outpatient CAP. Add macrolide or fluoroquinolone for severe CAP requiring ICU admission.

title: Thyroid Storm Diagnosis is Clinical — Don't Wait for TFTs
pearl: Thyroid storm (Burch-Wartofsky score ≥45 = probable storm) is a clinical diagnosis. Don't delay treatment waiting for TFT results. Start: propylthiouracil (PTU) or methimazole → wait 1 hour → then Lugol's iodine (blocks T4 release) → hydrocortisone + propranolol + supportive care.
explanation: PTU must be given BEFORE iodine because iodine provides substrate for more hormone synthesis. The 1-hour gap allows PTU to block organification first. PTU also blocks peripheral T4→T3 conversion. Lugol's iodine (SSKI) blocks further hormone release (Wolff-Chaikoff effect). Hydrocortisone covers relative adrenal insufficiency and also blocks T4→T3 conversion.

title: Febrile Seizure: Do Not Rush to CT or LP
pearl: Simple febrile seizures (age 6m-5y, duration <15 min, generalized, resolves spontaneously, no post-ictal deficit) do NOT require CT, MRI, or lumbar puncture in well-appearing children. Reassure parents, manage fever source, and explain 30-40% recurrence risk.
explanation: Risk of meningitis in a well-appearing child with simple febrile seizure is <0.2%. Indications for LP: age <12 months, meningeal signs, complex features, ill-appearing, previous antibiotics masking signs, first seizure in endemic area. EEG is NOT indicated acutely. Risk factors for recurrence: age <18 months, family history, fever <38.5°C at time of seizure, brief duration of fever before seizure.

title: Beta-Blockers Must NOT Be Started in Acute Decompensated Heart Failure
pearl: Beta-blockers reduce mortality in STABLE chronic HF — but they are CONTRAINDICATED in acute decompensated HF (wet and/or cold). Starting them during decomp increases mortality. If patient is already on beta-blocker and decompensates, reduce dose by 50% — do not stop abruptly.
explanation: In acute HF, sympathetic activation is compensatory and maintains cardiac output. Beta-blockade removes this compensation → worsening hemodynamics. Once stabilized (euvolemic, off IV diuretics, oral diuretics adequate), restart/initiate beta-blocker at low dose and uptitrate. Target: carvedilol, bisoprolol, or metoprolol succinate — not atenolol.

title: Oral Rehydration Therapy Works by Sodium-Glucose Cotransport — Not Glucose
pearl: ORS works because intestinal sodium-glucose cotransporter (SGLT1) remains functional even in severe secretory diarrhea. Glucose facilitates sodium absorption, which drives water absorption by osmosis. ORS is as effective as IV fluids for non-cholera diarrheal dehydration.
explanation: In cholera, although secretory diarrhea is massive, SGLT1 is unaffected. ORS exploits this: 1 Na + 1 glucose → cotransported, pulling water. WHO ORS (Na 75 mEq, Glucose 75 mmol, Cl 65 mEq, K 20 mEq, Citrate 10 mEq) is optimal. Rice-based ORS is slightly superior in cholera. IV fluids are only required for severe dehydration with shock.

title: Status Epilepticus: The 30-Minute Rule
pearl: Status epilepticus (SE) = seizure >5 min OR no recovery between seizures. Treat at 5 min, don't wait 30 min. Protocol: 0-5 min: IV lorazepam 0.1 mg/kg (max 4 mg) OR diazepam PR 0.3 mg/kg. 5-30 min if no response: IV phenytoin 20 mg/kg at ≤50 mg/min OR IV valproate 25-40 mg/kg. >30 min (refractory): ICU, midazolam infusion.
explanation: The '30-minute' definition is obsolete — treat at 5 minutes because SE becomes harder to abort with time (pharmacoresistance develops). Lorazepam is preferred over diazepam (longer duration of action). If no IV access: midazolam IM 0.1 mg/kg (buccal midazolam equally effective in children). Always check glucose (hypoglycemia is treatable cause). Phenytoin requires cardiac monitoring during infusion.

title: Vitamin K Deficiency vs. Liver Disease: Check Factor V
pearl: Factor V (labile factor) is made only in the liver, NOT vitamin K-dependent. Prolonged PT + prolonged APTT with LOW factor V = liver disease. Factor V NORMAL with prolonged PT = vitamin K deficiency or warfarin. This distinguishes the two without complex factor assays.
explanation: Vitamin K-dependent factors: II, VII, IX, X, Protein C and S. Factor V is also made in the liver but does not require vitamin K. In liver disease, ALL factors fall (including V). In vitamin K deficiency/warfarin, K-dependent factors fall but factor V remains normal. Practical: give vitamin K 10 mg IV and recheck INR in 6-12 hours — if it corrects fully, vitamin K deficiency confirmed.

title: Ramipril + Aldactone in Heart Failure: Monitor Potassium Closely
pearl: ACE inhibitor + spironolactone is evidence-based in HFrEF (RALES trial) — but this combination can cause life-threatening hyperkalemia. Check serum K+ and creatinine within 1 week of starting, then monthly for 3 months. Stop spironolactone if K+ >5.5 mEq/L.
explanation: ACE inhibitors reduce aldosterone → less K excretion. Spironolactone is also potassium-sparing. Combined effect can push K+ into dangerous range (>6.0 mEq/L → cardiac arrest risk). Risk is highest with renal impairment, diabetes, NSAIDs, or K+ supplements. After the RALES trial, spironolactone prescriptions increased — but so did hyperkalemia hospitalizations.

title: Leptospirosis: Weil's Disease is Not Just Jaundice + AKI
pearl: Weil's disease (severe leptospirosis) = jaundice + AKI + bleeding. But most leptospirosis presents as anicteric flu-like illness indistinguishable from dengue/malaria. Key clue: agricultural/animal exposure + conjunctival suffusion (red eyes without discharge) + calf muscle tenderness. Treat empirically with doxycycline in mild disease.
explanation: Leptospirosis is caused by Leptospira interrogans, transmitted via contact with contaminated water/soil (rats, cattle). Two phases: 4-7 day leptospiremic phase (flu-like) → 1-3 day improvement → 2nd immune phase (Weil's). Pulmonary hemorrhage syndrome (ARDS + hemoptysis) is the most lethal complication. Doxycycline 100 mg BD for 7 days (mild), IV penicillin/ceftriaxone for severe disease.

title: Think SIADH When Hyponatremia + Urine Osmolality >100 mOsm/kg
pearl: In hyponatremia: check urine osmolality first. If urine Osm <100 (dilute urine) = water excess/psychogenic polydipsia. If urine Osm >100 (inappropriately concentrated) = ADH effect. Then check urine sodium: if urine Na >20 mEq/L + euvolemic patient → SIADH. Restrict fluids to 800-1000 mL/day as initial treatment.
explanation: Hyponatremia diagnostic algorithm: first assess volume status (hypo, eu, hypervolemic). Euvolemic hyponatremia with high urine osmolality + urine Na >20 = SIADH. Causes: CNS disorders, pulmonary diseases, drugs (SSRIs, carbamazepine, cyclophosphamide), malignancy. Correct sodium slowly — no faster than 8-10 mEq/L/24h to prevent osmotic demyelination syndrome.

title: Statins: Give at Night for Maximum Efficacy
pearl: Hepatic cholesterol synthesis peaks at night (2-4 AM). Short-acting statins (simvastatin, pravastatin) should be taken at bedtime to coincide with peak enzyme activity. Long-acting statins (atorvastatin, rosuvastatin) can be taken any time of day.
explanation: Statins inhibit HMG-CoA reductase, which controls the rate-limiting step in cholesterol synthesis. Hepatic cholesterol synthesis follows a circadian rhythm with nocturnal peak. Short-acting statins (t½ <4h) must be given at night to catch this peak. Atorvastatin (t½ 14h) and rosuvastatin (t½ 19h) maintain inhibition throughout the day regardless of timing.

title: Organophosphate Poisoning: Atropine Until Secretions Dry — Not by Heart Rate
pearl: In OPC poisoning, atropine dose is titrated to DRYING OF SECRETIONS (lungs clear), NOT to heart rate or pupil size. Start 2-4 mg IV, double every 10 min until secretions dry. Some severe cases need 100s of mg over hours. After atropinization, add pralidoxime (only within 24-48 hours of exposure).
explanation: OPC inhibits acetylcholinesterase → ACh accumulation → muscarinic excess (SLUDGE/DUMBELS), nicotinic excess (muscle weakness, fasciculations), CNS (seizures). Atropine blocks only muscarinic receptors. The goal is clearing lung secretions (bronchospasm + hypersecretion → respiratory failure). Tachycardia is expected from atropine — don't underdose because of HR. Pralidoxime regenerates AChE if given early.

title: SpO2 Alone is NOT Enough in Carbon Monoxide Poisoning
pearl: Standard pulse oximetry CANNOT distinguish oxyhemoglobin from carboxyhemoglobin — both absorb light at the same wavelength (660 nm). SpO2 reads falsely normal (95-100%) in CO poisoning. Suspect CO whenever: enclosed space, multiple casualties, altered sensorium with normal SpO2. Diagnose with arterial blood gas (co-oximetry).
explanation: CO binds hemoglobin with 250x greater affinity than O2, forming carboxyhemoglobin (COHb). Standard pulse ox reads COHb as oxyhemoglobin → falsely reassuring SpO2. CO-oximeter measures COHb directly. Treatment: high-flow 100% O2 (reduces CO half-life from 5h → 90 min). Hyperbaric O2 if COHb >25%, neurological symptoms, pregnancy, or cardiac involvement.

title: Fluid Resuscitation in Pediatric DKA: Go Slow to Prevent Cerebral Edema
pearl: In pediatric DKA, avoid aggressive fluid boluses. Recommended: 10 mL/kg isotonic saline over 60 min (not the adult 500 mL in 15 min). Replace deficit over 48 hours (not 24h). Cerebral edema is the #1 cause of death in pediatric DKA and is precipitated by rapid osmolar shift.
explanation: Cerebral edema in pediatric DKA has 20-25% mortality. Risk factors: younger age, new-onset DKA, severe acidosis, high BUN, rapid fluid administration, aggressive bicarbonate use. Mechanism: rapid osmolar correction → relative brain intracellular hypertonicity → water moves in. Symptoms: headache, bradycardia, hypertension, falling GCS. Treatment: mannitol 0.5-1 g/kg IV or 3% saline.

title: Empiric TB Treatment: Never Start on Smear Alone
pearl: In India, never start anti-TB treatment on smear positivity alone without CBNAAT (Xpert MTB/RIF) to detect rifampicin resistance. If CBNAAT shows RR-TB, treating with standard HRZE is ineffective and allows further resistance amplification. CBNAAT is free at all NTEP centers.
explanation: India has the highest TB burden globally (>26% of world cases). Drug resistance is a major threat — RR-TB requires separate regimen (NTEP longer regimen or BPaL). CBNAAT simultaneously confirms M.tuberculosis AND detects rifampicin resistance as RR proxy. Results in 2 hours vs. 2 months for culture. For smear-negative TB: 2 samples on separate days, ideally with CBNAAT. NTEP provides DOTS, CBNAAT, and newer drugs free.
